💡Attempt each cropped question part first. Then use the button to reveal the supported answer and walkthrough.
🧪
Question 1 — Physical quantities and units
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q1(a)Q1(a) · Define force3.1.4[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Force is the rate of change of momentum.
✓Accept: \(F=\Delta p/\Delta t\), if accompanied by a clear definition.
How to think through it
1
This is the AS definition, not just \(F=ma\).
2
Use the exact phrase rate of change of momentum to access the mark directly.
Common trap
!Avoid defining force only as “a push or pull”; that is too IGCSE-level for this question.
Q1(b)Q1(b) · SI base units of force1.2.2 / 3.1.4[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Momentum has units \(\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-1}}\).
✓\(F=\Delta p/\Delta t\), so \([F]=\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-2}}\).
How to think through it
1
Start from \(F=\Delta p/\Delta t\).
2
Momentum is mass × velocity, so its unit is \(\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-1}}\).
3
Divide by time: \(\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-1}}/\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-2}}\).
Common trap
!Do not just write N. The question asks for SI base units.
Q1(c)Q1(c) · Homogeneity check1.2.3[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓LHS: \([v]=\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\).
✓RHS: \(\dfrac{Fk}{mA}=\dfrac{(\mathrm{kg\,m\,s^{-2}})(\mathrm{m^2\,s^{-1}})}{(\mathrm{kg})(\mathrm{m^2})}=\mathrm{m\,s^{-3}}\).
✓Since \(\mathrm{m\,s^{-3}}\ne\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\), the equation is not homogeneous.
How to think through it
1
Write the base units of every quantity before substituting.
2
Cancel units carefully on the right-hand side.
3
Compare the final units on both sides.
Common trap
!Students often say “not the same” without showing the units. Show both sides.
🧪
Question 2 — Momentum and upthrust
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q2(a)Q2(a) · Principle of conservation of momentum3.3.1[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓The total momentum of a system remains constant, provided no resultant external force acts on the system.
✓Equivalent wording: total momentum before = total momentum after, for an isolated system.
How to think through it
1
Mention the system.
2
Mention the condition: no resultant external force.
Common trap
!Do not say momentum is always conserved in a single object; it is the momentum of the system that is conserved.
Q2(b)(i)Q2(b)(i) · Speed after sticking collision3.3.2[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Use conservation of momentum: \((23)(3.4)=(160+23)v\).
✓\(v=0.427\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\), so \(v\approx0.43\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\).
How to think through it
1
The boat is initially stationary, so its initial momentum is zero.
2
The sack remains in contact with the boat, so they move together with common speed \(v\).
3
Solve \(23\times3.4=183v\).
Common trap
!Do not conserve kinetic energy; the objects stick together.
Q2(b)(ii)Q2(b)(ii) · Efficient kinetic energy ratio3.3.4[3]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Use conservation of momentum: \(m u=(m+M)v\).
✓For a sticking collision with one object initially stationary, \(\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}=\dfrac{m}{m+M}\).
✓\(\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}=\dfrac{23}{23+160}=\dfrac{23}{183}=0.126\).
Why the velocity does not matter
1
Let the sack have mass \(m\), the boat have mass \(M\), and the initial speed of the sack be \(u\). The boat is initially stationary.
2
Initial kinetic energy: \[E_1=\frac12 m u^2\]
3
Final kinetic energy: \[E_2=\frac12 (m+M)v^2\]
4
Momentum is conserved during the collision: \[m u=(m+M)v\] so \[v=\frac{m u}{m+M}\]
5
Substitute this into \(E_2\): \[E_2=\frac12 (m+M)\left(\frac{m u}{m+M}\right)^2=\frac12\frac{m^2u^2}{m+M}\]
6
Now divide by \(E_1\): \[\frac{E_2}{E_1}=\frac{\frac12\frac{m^2u^2}{m+M}}{\frac12 m u^2}=\frac{m}{m+M}\] The \(u^2\) term cancels, so the initial speed does not need to be calculated again.
Common trap
!This shortcut works here because the sack sticks to the boat and the boat is initially stationary. Do not use it automatically for every collision.
Q2(b)(iii)Q2(b)(iii) · Elastic or inelastic?3.3.3 / 3.3.4[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓The collision is inelastic.
✓Here \(E_2<E_1\), so total kinetic energy is not conserved. Some kinetic energy is transferred to other forms, such as internal energy and sound.
CAIE-style wording
1
Momentum is conserved because the external resistive forces during the collision are negligible.
2
However, kinetic energy is not conserved because the sack remains in contact with the mast and the calculated ratio \(E_2/E_1\) is less than 1.
Q2(c)(i)Q2(c)(i) · Show the upthrust is 1800 N4.3.6[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓At rest, upthrust equals total weight.
✓\(U=(160+23)g=(183)(9.81)=1.80\times10^3\,\mathrm{N}\).
How to think through it
1
The boat with sack is stationary, so vertical forces are balanced.
2
The upthrust must equal the combined weight.
Common trap
!Use the combined mass, not just the boat.
Q2(c)(ii)Q2(c)(ii) · Density of water4.3.1 / 4.3.6[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Convert volume: \(1.80\times10^5\,\mathrm{cm^3}=0.180\,\mathrm{m^3}\).
✓Use \(F=\rho gV\): \(\rho=\dfrac{1800}{(9.81)(0.180)}=1.02\times10^3\,\mathrm{kg\,m^{-3}}\).
How to think through it
1
Convert \(\mathrm{cm^3}\) to \(\mathrm{m^3}\) before substituting.
2
Rearrange Archimedes’ principle: \(\rho=F/(gV)\).
3
Give the answer to 3 significant figures.
Common trap
!\(1\,\mathrm{cm^3}=10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m^3}\), not \(10^{-2}\,\mathrm{m^3}\).
🧪
Question 3 — Energy and work
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q3(a)(i)Q3(a)(i) · Average resistive force5.1.1[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Work done against resistance: \(W=5100\,\mathrm{J}\).
✓\(F=W/s=5100/23=222\,\mathrm{N}\).
How to think through it
1
Use \(W=Fs\).
2
The distance along the track is \(23\,\mathrm{m}\).
3
Average force is work done divided by distance.
Common trap
!Use the track length, not the vertical height change.
Q3(a)(ii)Q3(a)(ii) · Change in height5.1 / 5.2[3]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Loss of kinetic energy: \(16.1-1.3=14.8\,\mathrm{kJ}\).
✓Gain in GPE: \(14.8-5.1=9.7\,\mathrm{kJ}\).
✓\(mg\Delta h=9700\), so \(\Delta h=\dfrac{9700}{(280)(9.81)}=3.53\,\mathrm{m}\).
How to think through it
1
The carriage loses kinetic energy as it moves upwards and works against resistance.
2
Only the energy remaining after resistive work becomes gravitational potential energy.
3
Use \(\Delta E_P=mg\Delta h\).
Common trap
!Do not use the full 14.8 kJ as GPE gain; 5.1 kJ is dissipated by resistive forces.
Q3(b)Q3(b) · Speed at Z compared with X5.1.2[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓The speed at Z is less than the speed at X.
✓Work is done against resistive forces, so some mechanical energy is transferred to other forms. Therefore the kinetic energy, and hence the speed, at Z is less than at X.
Why not just use \(v=\sqrt{2gh}\)?
1
The equation \(v=\sqrt{2gh}\) comes from \(mgh=\frac12mv^2\).
2
That only applies when gravitational potential energy is transferred completely into kinetic energy, for example when an object starts from rest and falls with no resistive forces.
3
In this question, the carriage already has kinetic energy at X and work is done against resistive forces. The mechanical energy is reduced, so the speed at Z is less than at X.
Common trap
!Do not ignore the phrase about work done against resistive forces. It tells you that kinetic energy is not fully recovered.
🧪
Question 4 — Moments, equilibrium and falling
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q4(a)(i)–(iii)Q4(a)(i)–(iii) · Moment and angle4.1 / 4.2[4]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Moment of a force about a point is \(\mathrm{force}\times\mathrm{perpendicular\ distance}\) from the point to the line of action of the force.
✓The moment of the weight about P is zero because the line of action of the weight passes through P / perpendicular distance is zero.
✓Vertical equilibrium: \(2T\sin\theta=19\).
✓\(\sin\theta=19/(2\times13)=0.731\), so \(\theta=46.9^\circ\approx47^\circ\).
How to think through it
1
The angle is measured from the horizontal.
2
Each tension has vertical component \(T\sin\theta\).
3
There are two identical tensions, so the upward resultant is \(2T\sin\theta\).
Common trap
!Use \(\sin\theta\), not \(\cos\theta\), because the angle is to the horizontal.
Q4(a)(iv)Q4(a)(iv) · Strain in wire6.1.5[3]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Cross-sectional area: \(A=\pi d^2/4=\pi(0.80\times10^{-3})^2/4=5.03\times10^{-7}\,\mathrm{m^2}\).
✓Stress: \(\sigma=F/A=13/(5.03\times10^{-7})=2.59\times10^7\,\mathrm{Pa}\).
✓Strain: \(\varepsilon=\sigma/E=\dfrac{2.59\times10^7}{9.3\times10^{10}}=2.8\times10^{-4}\).
How to think through it
1
Use the tension in one side of the wire: \(13\,\mathrm{N}\).
2
Calculate cross-sectional area from the diameter.
3
Since \(E=\mathrm{stress}/\mathrm{strain}\), rearrange to \(\mathrm{strain}=\mathrm{stress}/E\).
Common trap
!Strain has no unit.
Q4(b)(i)Q4(b)(i) · Time to hit ground2.1.7[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Use \(s=ut+\frac12at^2\), with \(u=0\), \(s=1.6\,\mathrm{m}\), \(a=g\).
✓\(t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2s}{g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3.2}{9.81}}=0.571\,\mathrm{s}\).
How to think through it
1
The bottom of the picture falls from rest through \(1.6\,\mathrm{m}\).
2
Air resistance is negligible, so acceleration is \(g\).
Common trap
!Use vertical motion only.
Q4(b)(ii)Q4(b)(ii) · Speed-distance graph2.1.7[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓The graph starts at the origin.
✓The curve increases with distance fallen, but the gradient decreases.
✓This is because \(v^2=2gs\), so \(v=\sqrt{2gs}\).
Speed-distance graph
1
Air resistance is negligible, so acceleration is constant at \(g\).
2
But the graph asked for is speed against distance, not speed against time.
3
Using \(v^2=u^2+2as\) with \(u=0\): \[v=\sqrt{2gs}\] This gives a curve, not a straight line.
Common trap
!A speed-time graph would be a straight line with gradient \(9.81\,\mathrm{m\ s}^{-2}\). A speed-distance graph is curved.
🧪
Question 5 — Waves and superposition
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q5(a)Q5(a) · Period and amplitude7.1.2[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Period: time taken for one complete oscillation / one complete cycle.
✓Amplitude: maximum displacement from the equilibrium position.
How to think through it
1
Both are definitions, so use precise wording.
2
For amplitude, include “maximum” and “from equilibrium”.
Common trap
!Do not define amplitude as the distance from crest to trough. That is twice the amplitude.
Q5(b)(i)–(iii)Q5(b)(i)–(iii) · Stationary wave on a string7.1 / 8.1[6]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Antinode: place the cross at the middle of any loop.
✓The diagram shows 3 loops, so \(0.96=3\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{2}\right)\) and \(\lambda=0.64\,\mathrm{m}\).
✓\(v=f\lambda=(25)(0.64)=16\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\).
✓At 30 Hz, \(\lambda=\dfrac{v}{f}=\dfrac{16}{30}=0.533\,\mathrm{m}\).
✓For a string fixed at both ends, \(L\) must be a whole-number multiple of \(\lambda/2\).
✓\(\dfrac{0.96}{0.533/2}=3.6\), not an integer, so a stationary wave is not formed.
How to think through it
1
A string fixed at both ends must have a node at each end.
2
The number of loops is the number of half-wavelengths.
3
When frequency changes but speed is constant, calculate the new wavelength.
4
Check whether the string length fits a whole number of half-wavelengths.
Common trap
!Use the fixed-end boundary condition first, then use \(v=f\lambda\).
Q5(c)(i)–(ii)Q5(c)(i)–(ii) · Diffraction grating8.4.1[4]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Grating spacing: \(d=1/(6.7\times10^5)=1.49\times10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m}\).
✓Use \(d\sin\theta=n\lambda\).
✓\(n=\dfrac{(1.49\times10^{-6})\sin 37^\circ}{4.5\times10^{-7}}=1.99\approx 2\).
✓With fewer lines per metre, \(d\) is larger, so \(\theta\) is smaller for the same order. The bright spots are closer together.
How to think through it
1
Convert lines per metre into grating spacing using \(d=\dfrac{1}{N}\).
2
Substitute into \(d\sin\theta=n\lambda\) and solve for \(n\).
3
Here \(n=1.99\), so round to the nearest whole order: \(n=2\). This is a measured angle for a visible bright spot, not a maximum-order calculation.
Common trap
!Round to the nearest integer in this case. Rounding down is used when calculating the maximum possible order, because the maximum order must not exceed \(d/\lambda\).
🧪
Question 6 — Electricity and circuits
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q6(a)Q6(a) · Electric current9.1.1[1]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Electric current is the rate of flow of charge.
✓Accept: \(I=\Delta Q/\Delta t\), with words.
How to think through it
1
At AS Level, mention charge, not just electrons.
2
“Flow of charge carriers” is also acceptable.
Common trap
!Do not say current is the same as voltage.
Q6(b)Q6(b) · Terminal p.d. graph10.1.5[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Use \(V=\mathcal{E}-Ir\).
✓At \(I=0\), \(V=1.50\,\mathrm{V}\).
✓At \(I=1.6\,\mathrm{A}\), \(V=1.50-(1.6)(0.75)=0.30\,\mathrm{V}\).
✓Draw a straight line from \((0,1.50)\) to \((1.6,0.30)\).
Graph interpretation
1
The equation \(V=\mathcal{E}-Ir\) has the form \(y=c+mx\).
2
The y-intercept is the e.m.f., \(\mathcal{E}=1.50\,\mathrm{V}\).
3
The gradient is \(-r\), so the magnitude of the gradient is the internal resistance: \(|m|=r=0.75\,\Omega\).
Common trap
!The terminal p.d. decreases as current increases because more energy is transferred per unit charge inside the cell.
Q6(c)(i)–(ii)Q6(c)(i)–(ii) · Resistivity and external resistor9.3 / 10.2[5]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Resistivity: \(\rho=\dfrac{RA}{L}=\dfrac{(0.82)(2.0\times10^{-7})}{6.8}=2.4\times10^{-8}\,\Omega\,\mathrm{m}\).
✓Terminal p.d. across the external circuit: \(V=\mathcal{E}-Ir=1.50-(1.8)(0.75)=0.15\,\mathrm{V}\).
✓Total external resistance: \(R_{\mathrm{external}}=V/I=0.15/1.8=0.0833\,\Omega\).
✓Wire X and resistor \(R\) are in parallel: \(\dfrac{1}{0.0833}=\dfrac{1}{0.82}+\dfrac{1}{R}\), so \(R=0.0928\,\Omega\approx0.093\,\Omega\).
How to think through it
1
For resistivity, rearrange \(R=\rho L/A\) to \(\rho=RA/L\).
2
For the circuit, do not use \(1.50\,\mathrm{V}\) across the external resistors. The cell has internal resistance, so the terminal p.d. is smaller.
3
The current in the cell is the total current supplied to the parallel combination of wire X and resistor \(R\).
Common trap
!The \(0.82\,\Omega\) wire and resistor \(R\) are in parallel, not in series.
Q6(c)(iii)Q6(c)(iii) · Power in internal resistance9.2.3 / 10.1.5[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓Use \(P=I^2r\).
✓\(P=(1.8)^2(0.75)=2.43\,\mathrm{W}\).
How to think through it
1
The current through the internal resistance is the current in the cell.
2
Use \(r=0.75\,\Omega\), not the external resistor.
Common trap
!Do not use terminal p.d. here; the question asks for the power dissipated in the internal resistance.
🧪
Question 7 — Particle physics
Click each button after attempting the question.
Q7(a)Q7(a) · Structure of a nuclear atom11.1.2[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓An atom has a small, dense, positively charged nucleus containing protons and neutrons.
✓Electrons orbit / surround the nucleus. Most of the atom is empty space.
How to think through it
1
Mention the nucleus.
2
Mention protons, neutrons and electrons.
3
For a fuller answer, include that the nucleus is small compared with the atom.
Common trap
!Do not say electrons are inside the nucleus.
Q7(b)Q7(b) · Decay types from proton number change11.1.7 / 11.1.11[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓P has 94 protons and becomes 92 protons, so proton number decreases by 2: alpha decay.
✓Q has 91 protons and becomes 92 protons, so proton number increases by 1: beta-minus decay.
How to think through it
1
In \(\alpha\)-decay, proton number decreases by 2.
2
In \(\beta^-\)-decay, a neutron changes into a proton, so proton number increases by 1.
Common trap
!Do not use \(\beta^+\) for Q; \(\beta^+\) would decrease the proton number by 1.
Q7(c)Q7(c) · Identify particle X11.1.7 / 11.1.8[2]
Suggested answer / mark points
✓\(\dfrac{e}{m_e}=\dfrac{1.60\times10^{-19}}{9.11\times10^{-31}}=1.76\times10^{11}\,\mathrm{C\,kg^{-1}}\).
✓The magnitude matches an electron/positron.
✓The ratio is positive, so particle X is a positron.
How to think through it
1
Recognise the charge-to-mass ratio magnitude.
2
Then use the sign of the charge-to-mass ratio.
3
A positron has the same mass as an electron but positive charge.
Common trap
!If you write electron, you have ignored the positive sign.

Relevant formulae and relationships

These are the formulae and relationships used in this paper.

IdeaFormulaUse
Force and momentum\(F=\dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}\)Force is the rate of change of momentum.
Momentum\(p=mv\)Useful for the sticking collision in Q2.
Kinetic energy\(E_K=\dfrac12mv^2\)Used for collision energy and energy changes.
Energy ratio shortcut\(\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}=\dfrac{m}{m+M}\)Only for this type of sticking collision with one object initially stationary.
Work done\(W=Fs\)Force × displacement in the direction of the force.
GPE change\(\Delta E_P=mg\Delta h\)Used for height change in Q3.
Uniform acceleration\(s=ut+\dfrac12at^2\), \(v^2=u^2+2as\)Used for falling motion and speed-distance graph.
Speed-distance from rest\(v=\sqrt{2gs}\)For falling from rest with negligible air resistance; gives a curved speed-distance graph.
Moment\(\mathrm{moment}=F\times d_\perp\)Perpendicular distance from pivot to line of action.
Young modulus\(E=\dfrac{\mathrm{stress}}{\mathrm{strain}}\), \(\mathrm{stress}=F/A\)Strain is dimensionless.
Wave speed\(v=f\lambda\)Used for progressive waves on the string.
Diffraction grating\(d\sin\theta=n\lambda\)Use \(d=\dfrac{1}{N}\), where \(N\) is lines per metre.
Terminal p.d.\(V=\mathcal{E}-Ir\)Straight-line graph; gradient \(=-r\), so \(|m|=r\).
Resistivity\(R=\rho L/A\)For exam use: resistivity is the product of the resistance and cross-sectional area per unit length of a material, so \(\rho=RA/L\).
Electrical power / power dissipation\(P=I^2R\)Use for power dissipated in a resistor, including power dissipated in the internal resistance of a cell.
Charge-to-mass ratio\(q/m\)Sign matters: positive ratio means positive charge.

Key vocabulary

English, Thai, Chinese and Korean support for the key ideas in the paper.

1. Mechanics, momentum and energy
EnglishThaiChineseKoreanMeaning / exam use
forceแรง力힘Rate of change of momentum.
momentumโมเมนตัม动量운동량\(p=mv\), a vector quantity.
conservation of momentumการอนุรักษ์โมเมนตัม动量守恒운동량 보존Total momentum remains constant if no resultant external force acts.
inelastic collisionการชนแบบไม่ยืดหยุ่น非弹性碰撞비탄성 충돌Kinetic energy is not conserved.
upthrustแรงพยุง浮力부력Upward force on an object in a fluid.
densityความหนาแน่น密度밀도Mass per unit volume.
work doneงานที่ทำ做功한 일Force × displacement in the direction of the force.
gravitational potential energyพลังงานศักย์โน้มถ่วง重力势能중력 위치 에너지Energy due to height: \(\Delta E_P=mg\Delta h\).
kinetic energyพลังงานจลน์动能운동 에너지Energy due to motion: \(E_K=\frac12mv^2\).
2. Equilibrium, materials and motion graphs
EnglishThaiChineseKoreanMeaning / exam use
moment of a forceโมเมนต์ของแรง力矩힘의 모멘트Turning effect about a point.
equilibriumสมดุล平衡평형No resultant force and no resultant torque.
stressความเค้น应力응력Force per unit cross-sectional area.
strainความเครียด应变변형률Extension divided by original length; no unit.
Young modulusมอดูลัสของยัง杨氏模量영률Ratio of stress to strain.
negligible air resistanceแรงต้านอากาศน้อยมากจนละเลยได้空气阻力可忽略공기 저항 무시 가능Acceleration due to gravity may be taken as constant.
3. Waves and superposition
EnglishThaiChineseKoreanMeaning / exam use
periodคาบ周期주기Time for one complete oscillation.
amplitudeแอมพลิจูด振幅진폭Maximum displacement from equilibrium.
wavelengthความยาวคลื่น波长파장Distance between adjacent points in phase.
stationary waveคลื่นนิ่ง驻波정상파A stationary wave is formed by the superposition of two progressive waves of the same frequency and amplitude travelling in opposite directions, producing fixed nodes and antinodes with no net transfer of energy.
nodeโหนด波节마디Point of zero displacement in a stationary wave.
antinodeแอนติโนด波腹배Point of maximum displacement.
diffraction gratingเกรตติงเลี้ยวเบน衍射光栅회절 격자Many equally spaced slits/lines used to separate light.
orderอันดับ级次차수The integer \(n\) in \(d\sin\theta=n\lambda\).
4. Electricity, circuits and particle physics
EnglishThaiChineseKoreanMeaning / exam use
electric currentกระแสไฟฟ้า电流전류Rate of flow of charge.
terminal p.d.ความต่างศักย์ปลายขั้ว端电压단자 전압Potential difference across the terminals of a cell.
internal resistanceความต้านทานภายใน内阻내부 저항Resistance inside a source of e.m.f.; \(|\mathrm{gradient}|=r\) for \(V\) against \(I\).
resistivityสภาพต้านทานไฟฟ้า电阻率비저항For exam use: the product of the resistance and cross-sectional area per unit length of a material, \(\rho=RA/L\).
parallel circuitวงจรขนาน并联电路병렬 회로Potential difference is the same across each branch.
nuclear atomอะตอมที่มีนิวเคลียส核式原子핵 원자Small positive nucleus with electrons around it.
positronโพซิตรอน正电子양전자Antiparticle of the electron; same mass, positive charge.
